title: From the fine spatialization of urban data in Africa to unidentified geographical objects
markdown:
By [**Hervé Gazel**](https://e-geopolis.org/equipe/herve-gazel/), [**François Moriconi-Ébrard**] (https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Moriconi-Ebrard), [**Jose Luis San Emeterio**] (https://etudes-africaines.cnrs.fr/annuaire-des-chercheurs/jose-luis-san-emeterio/), 2021

**_Topic:**_  Urbanisation

**_Keywords:**_ Urbanisation, Mega agglomerations

**_Africapolis data:**_ GIS, agglomeration data

**_Link to the original article:**_ [Geographical Space](hhttps://www.cairn.info/revue-espace-geographique-2021-3-page-214.htm)
___


Africa is undergoing rapid urbanisation, transforming landscapes and societies. This phenomenon presents unique challenges and opportunities for development, necessitating detailed study to guide public sector action.
Recent investigations using Africapolis have led to the discovery of a new type of urban agglomeration, the Unidentified Geographic Object (UGO). UGOs are large agglomerations with at least 500 000 inhabitants that are seldom identified in official statistics (Figure 1) (Table 1). 

##### Table 1: Sixteen UGO agglomerations with more than 500,000 inhabitants in Sub-Saharan Africa in 2015

| Name of agglomeration | Altitude (metres) | Country | Population of agglomeration | Population of largest centre | Area (km2) | Number of local administrative units | Density (people/km2) |
|------------------------|--------------------|---------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|------------|--------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| Onitsha                | 98                 | Nigeria | 8,531,000                    | 176,200                       | 2,867      | 46                                   | 2,976                 |
| Aba                    | 83                 | Nigeria | 1,687,000                    | 136,000                       | 754        | 14                                   | 2,237                 |
| Nsukka                 | 461                | Nigeria | 1,430,000                    | 390,525                       | 699        | 9                                    | 2,047                 |
| Uyo                    | 79                 | Nigeria | 2,271,000                    | 414,600                       | 997        | 22                                   | 2,277                 |
| Bafoussam              | 1,609              | Cameroon| 1,146,000                    | 248,377                       | 1,318      | 43                                   | 607                   |
| Sodo                   | 1,664              | Ethiopia| 2,262,000                    | 145,100                       | 1,930      | 318                                  | 1,172                 |
| Awassa                 | 1,744              | Ethiopia| 2,183,000                    | 300,100                       | 1,302      | 236                                  | 1,677                 |
| Kisumu                 | 1,645              | Kenya   | 5,040,000                    | 297,000                       | 5,863      | 655                                  | 860                   |
| Kisii                  | 1,470              | Kenya   | 3,407,000                    | 72,000                        | 5,001      | 466                                  | 681                   |
| Embu                   | 1,187              | Kenya   | 2,047,000                    | 39,000                        | 1,555      | 361                                  | 1,317                 |
| Maua                   | 1,299              | Kenya   | 848,000                      | 17,000                        | 943        | 137                                  | 899                   |
| Bomet                  | 1,875              | Kenya   | 753,000                      | 136,000                       | 1,504      | 179                                  | 501                   |
| Meru                   | 740                | Kenya   | 515,000                      | 42,000                        | 499        | 115                                  | 1,032                 |
| Mbale                  | 1,946              | Uganda, Kenya | 2,229,000                | 98,746                        | 1,060      | 125                                  | 2,109                 |
| Busia                  | 1,163              | Rwanda, Uganda | 613,000                    | 57,354                        | 366        | 43                                   | 1,675                 |
| Gisenyi-Kosoro         | 2,529              | D.R. Congo | 1,255,000                  | 21,348                        | 355        | 48                                   | 3,534                 |
| Ruhango                | 1,712              | Rwanda   | 600,000                      | 70,817                        | 198        | 18                                   | 3,037                 |

######*Source: Africapolis, 2018*

##### *Figure 1. Localisation of UGO agglomerations and other agglomerations with more than 500,000 inhabitants in Sub-Saharan Africa in 2015*
![img](/images/tempimages/Geopolis_1.jpg)

These agglomerations often result from in-situ urbanisation in densely populated rural areas. The emergence of these geographical objects leads us to question the process of their genesis, which can be summarized as follows: an expanding demographic universe in a confined geographic territory. This research characterized these agglomerations and uncovered five common characteristics: 

####An absence of an urban “center”

UGOs extend over numerous local administrative units (167 on average). The central city, typically the largest urban entity in an agglomeration, only contains a small fraction of the population of a UGO, about 7.3%, which is notably lower compared to the 66.5% average in other cities of sub-Saharan Africa with populations exceeding half a million. In these agglomerations, multiple small centers, officially considered urban, are interspersed with large stretches of unofficially urbanized area. Each exerts influence over just a segment of the whole agglomeration, preventing the emergence of a single urban centre.

####A moderate urban population density but too high to be rural

The second characteristic is that the density demographic within the agglomeration is, on average, 4.4 times lower than that of other urban areas in sub-Saharan Africa with more than 500 000 inhabitants (table 2). 
Table 2: Characteristics of sixteen UGO agglomerations with more than 500,000 inhabitants compared with other agglomerations with more than 500,000 inhabitants in Sub-Saharan Africa
 	
#####Table 2: Summary of UGO agglomerations and other agglomerations

|                                | Number of agglomerations | Area (km2) | Population of agglomeration | Density (people/km2) | Population of largest centre | Largest centre share of agglomeration population (%) | Average altitude (metres) |
|--------------------------------|--------------------------|-------------|------------------------------|-----------------------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **UGO agglomerations**         | 17                       | 27,376      | 36,817,068                   | 1,345                 | 2,696,661                     | 7.3                                                 | 1,347                     |
| **Other agglomerations**       |                          |             |                              |                       |                               |                                                     |                           |
| Coastal                        | 30                       | 9,098       | 54,754,469                   | 6,018                 | 41,664,254                    | 76.1                                                | 37                        |
| Non-coastal                    | 63                       | 21,360      | 116,262,608                  | 5,443                 | 72,318,436                    | 62.2                                                | 813                       |
| **Total**                      | 93                       | 30,281      | 169,997,257                  | 5,614                 | 1,131,006,007                 | 66.5                                                | 559                       |
| **All agglomerations**         | 110                      | 57,657      | 206,814,325                  | 3,587                 | 115,802,668                   | 56.0                                                | 696                       |

######*Source: Africapolis, 2018*

However, the population density is equivalent, or even superior to that of many North American and some European urban agglomerations of similar size. The metropolitan areas of Atlanta (United States) and Kisii (Kenya) have very similar densities (659 versus 686 inhabitants/km²) (table 3). These densities are, therefore, closer to urban than rural values.

#####Table 3: Density of various urban agglomerations outside of Africa in 2010

| Agglomeration               | Population  | Area (km2) | Density (people/km2) | Comparable to:    |
|-----------------------------|-------------|------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Atlanta (United States)     | 4,515,419   | 6,851      | 659                  | Kisii (Kenya)    |
| Los Angeles (United States) | 15,272,268  | 6,844      | 2,232                | Onitsha (Nigeria)|
| Lyon (France)               | 1,650,890   | 666        | 2,477                | Mbale (Uganda)   |
| Venice (Italy)              | 2,976,711   | 2,524      | 1,180                | Meru (Kenya)     |

######*Source: Africapolis, 2018*

####A continental and high-altitude location

The third characteristic is linked to their location: no UGO is located on the coast. Since the colonial era, the urbanisation of Africa, notably sub-Saharan, has been considered as an essentially coastal phenomenon. 
With an average altitude of 1347 meters, the UGOs are clearly participating in a new movement: that of urbanisation from the interior regions, particularly Nigeria and the African highlands. Already noted in Brazil [_Moriconi-Ébrard, 2019_](https://journals.openedition.org/confins/20060), a topographically “descending” logic – from the summits towards the valleys, and from the interior towards the coast – is opposed to the “up-and-down” appropriation – of the valleys towards the summits, from the coast towards the interior – characteristic of processes of colonial urbanisation.


####A traditionally scattered regional population

The fourth characteristic of sub-Saharan UGOs is to have emerged in regions not only without cities, but also without villages. The most emblematic example is that of Biafra. In the Ibibio, Ibo, and Tiv countries of [southeastern Nigeria](https://africapolis.org/en/explore?agglomeration=Onitsha&country=Nigeria&poprange=1,2,3,4,5,6&year=2020) (present-day Anambra, Imo, Abia, Ebonyi, and Kogi states), where the traditional habitat is scattered in clearings, fields, and grasslands along paths and trails. In UGOs, the propensity for habitat dispersal appears to be particularly enduring.  


####Prodigious demographic vitality
Since the early 1960s continuous and relatively high-density settlement has been observed in some particularly fertile areas of Africa. These areas are the same ones that are now hosting UGOs. Over the past five decades densities in these areas have multiplied by 4.6, transforming scattered continuous high-density habitats into urban agglomerations. As demographic and economic growth continues, there is little chance that this type of settlement will become rural again. The emergence of UGOs is therefore accompanied by a fifth characteristic: strong and persistent demographic growth, sufficient to counterbalance migration. 


####Conclusion
In UGOs, population growth combined with the scarcity of buildable land leads to the densification of buildings in a combination of grouped and linear forms, eventually leading to a transformation in the form of the built-environment and the emergence of agglomerations that bear little resemblance to conventional notions of a city, and yet are plainly not rural.
The detection of UGOs was enabled by Africapolis’ use of a single operational definition of the urban agglomerations and a rigorous method. The application of the definition can highlight situations that were unknown, unsuspected, and therefore difficult to insert into the academic narrative, such as the presence of UGOs. Future research will delve deeper into these phenomena, refining our understanding of urbanisation in Africa.

### Authors' Biography

Hervé Gazel, geographer, specialist in cartography and the visualization of geographic, geostatistical, and geo-historical data. Associate professor at the Faculty of Humanities, Letters, and Societies, Lyon 3 University. Affiliated researcher at the [e-Geopolis Institute](https://e-geopolis.org/equipe/herve-gazel/).

François Moriconi-Ébrard, research director at the French National Centre for Scientific Research (CNRS), University of Paris, Interdisciplinary Laboratory of Tomorrow’s Energies (LIED), and president of the e-Geopolis Institute.

José Luis San Emeterio, affiliated researcher at the e-Geopolis Institute.
